draft.introduction: The Memorabilia of Xenophon (c. 370 BCE) presents Socrates through the eyes of a soldier and practical man, emphasizing his piety, self-control, and usefulness to friends. It offers a portrait of Socrates strikingly different from Plato’s philosophical dialogues.

translation.text: I have often wondered by what arguments those who indicted Socrates could have persuaded the Athenians that he deserved death from the state. For the indictment against him was something like this: Socrates is guilty of not acknowledging the gods the city acknowledges, and of introducing other new divinities. He is also guilty of corrupting the youth.
translation.original_text: πολλάκις ἐθαύμασα τίσι ποτὲ λόγοις Ἀθηναίους ἔπεισαν

translation.text: He himself never ceased examining what each existing thing is. What is pious? What is impious? What is noble? What is shameful? What is just? What is unjust? What is prudence? What is madness? What is courage? What is cowardice? What is a city? What is a statesman? These and similar questions he considered, believing that those who know these things are noble and good, while those who are ignorant of them deserve to be called slaves.
translation.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ περὶ τῶν ἀνθρωπίνων ἀεὶ διελέγετο

translator_notes.note: Xenophon’s Socrates is a practical moralist who examines ethical concepts to make people better, not a metaphysician seeking transcendent Forms. This more down-to-earth Socrates corrects the sometimes otherworldly impression left by Plato and reminds us that multiple legitimate portraits of any great thinker are possible.
